CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

Most of the collection is Chinese (about 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (about 600 pieces), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. This makes sense because the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) was founded in 1602. Starting in the 1600s, thin, hard porcelain with blue Chinese decorations became super popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain became a big part of Dutch home decor.

Since 1952, the Rijksmuseum has also housed the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes different types of Asian art: Japanese tea ceramics, Chinese Song dynasty bowls and Tang dynasty funeral sculptures, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.

Around the same time, a civil war in China (from 1644 to 1684) stopped almost all trade in Chinese porcelain. This gave Delft potters a big chance. Their earthenware became very popular with rich people in Europe, and the style really took off.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.

In the course of the 17th century, painters chose a somewhat looser arrangement, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Shooters by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. persona1_adapted:
**Back in the 1600s, cities in the Netherlands had their own security. Instead of a police force, they had groups of citizens called the civic guard. These were regular people—mostly men—who had to join if they were between 18 and 60 years old. But there was a catch: you had to buy your own weapons!**

**To show off their group, the guards would pay for a big group portrait. Each member chipped in money, so these paintings were expensive. The earliest ones, from the 1500s, were pretty stiff and boring—everyone just stood still. But by the 1600s, artists started making them more lively and interesting. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's *Banquet of the Marksmen*.**

**And it wasn't just the guards who wanted group portraits. Other city groups, clubs, and even rich families also paid artists to paint them together.**

Over the 17th century, artists adopted looser compositions, with Rembrandt’s *Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst’s lively *Banquet of the Marksmen* as key highlights. In the 1600s, members of local city militias—called "marksmen"—were often painted together in group portraits. Back then, powerful cities handled their own security. These group portraits were expensive projects because each militiaman paid for his own likeness to be included. The earliest ones, from the 1500s, look quite stiff and formal. But as the 1600s went on, artists started making the compositions feel more relaxed and lively. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's *Banquet of the Marksmen*.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.

For instance, Rachel Ruysch mainly painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed making arrangements with food and eating and drinking tools. CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

The first genre paintings appeared in the 1500s in Flanders (a region in Europe). But in the Netherlands, this type of art didn't become popular until about 100 years later. Famous Dutch artists like Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster made genre paintings famous. Their works often had a hidden joke or a secret moral lesson.

The name "genre painting" wasn't used until the 1800s, when this style was at its most popular. The Rijksmuseum museum in Amsterdam has genre paintings from that time too, including works by Jozef Israëls. He painted lively scenes of farmers and fishermen going about their daily lives.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.

Whoever holds the right to make coins chooses the look, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver moved mostly locally, because once abroad, the metal value was most important. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large part of the silver that Spain got from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. persona1_adapted:
**What Makes Money Valuable?**

Have you ever wondered why a coin that costs just a few cents to make can be worth 2 euros? It’s all because of the stamp on it. Whoever has the right to make coins gets to decide what they look like, how much metal they contain, and what they’re worth.

For hundreds of years, gold and silver coins traveled all over the world through trade. Coins that had very little gold or silver in them usually stayed close to home. Why? Because if you took them to another country, people cared more about the actual metal in the coin than what it said on the front.

The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was a major center for trading precious metals. Even during the Eighty Years’ War, a lot of the silver that Spain took from the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. And until the late 1700s, huge “silver fleets” of ships sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, that money traveled everywhere—even all the way to Asia with the Dutch East India Company (the VOC).

---

**Adapted Text:**

What is money actually worth? Why does a small metal coin, worth only a few euro cents as a material, suddenly have a much higher value simply because it is stamped with the number '2 euro'? The person or institution that has the right to mint coins decides three things: the design, the amount of precious metal used, and the official value.

For centuries, gold and silver coins traveled across the world through trade. Coins that contained very little gold or silver were mostly used in local areas. This is because, when a coin was taken to a foreign country, the value of the metal itself became the most important factor.

The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was, for a long time, a central place for trading precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War (a Dutch war of independence from Spain, 1568–1648), a large part of the silver that Spain took from its colonies in the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, large groups of ships, called "silver fleets," sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money then traveled to other parts of the world, for example to Asia, through the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

For a long time, the Amsterdam Exchange Bank was a major center for trading precious metals. Even during the Eighty Years' War, a lot of the silver that Spain got from its colonies in the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 1700s, large fleets of ships carrying silver sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled all over the world—for example, to Asia through the Dutch East India Company (the VOC).

The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.

persona1_adapted:
**Who owned this object before it came to the museum?**  
The people who owned it—their lives, relationships, and stories—can tell us a lot about why the object matters.  

By doing something called *provenance research* (which means tracing an object’s history), we can learn about the people who owned it before World War II. This reminds us that many of those people were persecuted (treated unfairly and cruelly) during the Holocaust.  

Even if an object wasn’t stolen, its history still gives us clues about the past. The Rijksmuseum’s collection mostly tells the stories of collectors from 1933 to 1945. But that doesn’t give us the full picture of everyone who suffered. Why? Because many everyday objects never ended up in museums, or they’re harder to track down.  

Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum help us remember how diverse (full of different kinds of people and cultures) Europe was before the war. Provenance research shows that the people the Nazis targeted were active members of Dutch culture and history.  

Today, these objects are silent witnesses. They can’t speak, but they still have stories to tell.

The Rijksmuseum collection mainly tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Therefore, research into the collection cannot provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. Many everyday objects never entered museums, or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums remain silent witnesses to this today.
persona3_adapted:
**Who owned this object before it came to the museum?**  
The lives, relationships, and backgrounds of its owners can tell us a lot about why the object mattered. By studying an object’s history—called “provenance”—we can uncover stories of people who owned it before World War II. This reminds us of those who were persecuted during the Holocaust.  

Even if an object was never stolen, knowing where it came from helps us understand its past. The Rijksmuseum’s collection mostly includes objects from collectors who lived between 1933 and 1945. So, researching the collection can’t give us a complete picture of everyone who was persecuted. Many everyday items never ended up in museums, or are harder to trace.  

Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us how diverse Europe was before the war. Provenance research shows that the people targeted by the Nazis were active contributors to Dutch culture and history. Today, objects in museums remain silent witnesses to that past.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.

**Sculpture at the Rijksmuseum: A World in 3D**

Sculpture is all around us—from ancient statues to modern art. At the Rijksmuseum, the collection of European sculpture is huge and includes works made from many different materials, from marble to metal.

This online catalog is a brand-new guide to every single European sculpture in the museum’s collection. The last time a full catalog was published was way back in 1973. Since then, art historians have learned a lot more about these sculptures, and the museum has added many new pieces. So it was time for a big update!

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions, like the Royal Cabinet of Rarities) didn’t start collecting sculptures in a serious way until the late 1800s. Before that, there were only a few. One early example is a marble bust of a man named Andries de Graeff, made by the artist Artus Quellinus. The Dutch government bought it in 1817.

At first, the museum mostly wanted sculptures that were important to Dutch history. But in the 1900s, that changed. Two museum leaders—Adriaan Pit (director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (a curator who made the 1973 catalog)—worked hard to bring in sculptures from other countries. Today, about half of the collection comes from outside the Netherlands.

The international side of the collection got a big boost in 1952. That’s when the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of Italian and German sculptures that had belonged to a man named Fritz Mannheimer. These works were recovered from Germany after World War II. This addition helped the museum keep growing its collection of art from all over Europe.

**About the Sculpture Collection**

This online catalog explores the Rijksmuseum's vast collection of European sculpture, from the Middle Ages to today. It covers sculptures of every size and shape, made from a wide range of materials.

The catalog includes every European sculpture owned by the museum. The last time a complete guide to the collection was published was in 1973. That book was very useful, but it's now out of date. This international focus got another big boost in 1952. That year, the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of a group of Italian and German sculptures. These pieces had once belonged to a collector named Fritz Mannheimer and were recovered from Germany after World War II. This important addition encouraged the museum to keep buying art from around the world.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.

**Notes on adaptation:**

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
lexical_adapted: The Fishing for Souls: a story of the jealousy of the different religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river scene, large groups of people are placed on both sides of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their backers: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the young Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the front sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Faith, Hope, and Charity. On the right side stand the Catholics and their backers: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the guidance of a bishop.
persona1_adapted:
**Fishing for Souls: A Story of Rivalry During a Time of Peace**

This painting is like a puzzle. It shows a scene from a time called the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621), when the Dutch Republic and Spain paused their long war. But even though they weren't fighting, there was still a lot of tension—especially about religion.

In the picture, you see a river with people on both sides. There are boats on the water, and a rainbow stretches across the sky. But this isn't just a pretty landscape. Every part of the painting has a hidden meaning.

**On the left side** are the **Protestants** (a branch of Christianity). They have powerful friends, like the Princes of Orange, the King of England, and the young King of France. In the big boat in front, Protestant fishermen are casting nets. Their nets have labels that say "Faith," "Hope," and "Love"—important ideas for them.

**On the right side** are the **Catholics** (another branch of Christianity). Their supporters include the rulers of the Spanish Netherlands, a famous general, and even the Pope (carried by church leaders). In their small boat, priests are fishing too, led by a bishop.

So what's the point? The "fishing" is a metaphor. Both sides are trying to "catch" people's souls—to win them over to their religion. The painting shows how jealous and competitive these groups were, even during a time of peace. It's a snapshot of a world divided by belief, but also a reminder that people have always argued about what's true.
persona2_adapted:
Here is the adapted version of the text, tailored for a non-native English speaker with deep art knowledge.

---

**Adapted Text:**

*Fishing for Souls* is an allegorical painting that represents the religious tensions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. The scene takes place in a river landscape, with large groups of people gathered on both banks. Several rowboats are on the water, and a rainbow appears on the horizon.

On the left side, we see the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry; Frederick V of the Palatinate; James I, King of England; and the young Louis XIII, King of France, together with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground, Protestant fishermen are at work. Their nets are marked with the words *Fides* (Faith), *Spes* (Hope), and *Charitas* (Charity).

On the right side, the Catholics and their supporters are shown: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is carried by cardinals. In the smaller Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

---

**Notes for the reader:**

- *Allegory*: A story or image in which characters or events represent abstract ideas or moral qualities.
- *Twelve Years' Truce*: A temporary peace (1609–1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain during the Eighty Years' War.
- *Fides, Spes, Charitas*: Latin for Faith, Hope, and Charity—the three theological virtues in Christian tradition.
- *Princes of Orange*: Members of the Dutch House of Orange-Nassau, key leaders in the Dutch struggle for independence.
- *Frederick V of the Palatinate*: A German prince and a key Protestant figure in the early 17th century.
- *Archdukes Albert and Isabella*: The Habsburg rulers of the Spanish Netherlands.
- *General Ambrosio Spinola*: A Spanish-Italian military commander for Spain.
- *Cardinals*: High-ranking officials in the Catholic Church.

On the left side of the river are the Protestants and their allies. You can see important leaders like the Princes of Orange (Maurice and Frederick Henry), Frederick V of the Palatinate, King James I of England, and the young King Louis XIII of France with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat closest to us, Protestant fishermen are at work. Their nets are labeled with the Latin words *Fides* (Faith), *Spes* (Hope), and *Charitas* (Charity).

On the right side of the river are the Catholics and their supporters. This group includes Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is being carried by cardinals. In a small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

The rainbow on the horizon adds a hopeful note, but the main message of the painting is about the jealousy and competition between different religious groups during this uneasy peace.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
lexical_adapted: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona1_adapted:
This kimono was made for a young, unmarried woman. It’s called a *furisode*, which means “swinging sleeves.” The sleeves are extra long and loose.

The whole kimono is covered with over 200 flying cranes. In Japanese culture, cranes are a symbol of good luck and long life.

The kimono is made of black silk crepe, a fabric with a bumpy texture. The white, red, and gray crane designs were created using a special dyeing technique called *yuzen*. Tiny bits of shiny gold foil add sparkle.

The inside is lined with red silk. On the back, there is a single bamboo crest, which was the family symbol of the woman who owned it.

**Adapted Text:**

A single bamboo crest (mon) appears on the garment.
persona3_adapted: This kimono was worn by a young unmarried woman on formal occasions. It is covered with over two hundred flying cranes, a symbol of good luck and long life. The kimono is made from black silk crepe, a fabric with a slightly crinkled texture. The design was created using a special dyeing technique called yuzen, which allows for detailed patterns. The colors are white, red, and grey, with small accents of gold foil for extra shine. The inside lining is red silk. On the back, there is a single bamboo crest, which may represent the woman’s family.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
lexical_adapted: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the front, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona1_adapted:
**A View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil**

This painting shows the Brazilian town of Olinda when the Dutch controlled it. On the left, you can see a hill with a group of people standing near the ruins of a church. In the front of the painting, the artist included lots of tropical plants, fruits, and animals you’d find in the region—like an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil* depicts a scene from the colonial period. On the left, a hill shows a group of people standing near the ruins of a church. In the foreground, the artist includes tropical plants, fruits, and several animals: an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard.

**Notes for the reader:**
- "Dutch Brazil" refers to the period (1630–1654) when the Dutch Republic controlled parts of northeastern Brazil, including Olinda.
- "Foreground" means the part of the painting closest to the viewer.
- The inclusion of exotic animals and plants was common in Dutch colonial art to emphasize the richness and novelty of the New World.
persona3_adapted: On the left side of this painting, you can see a hill with a small group of people standing near the ruins of a church. In the front of the scene, there are tropical plants and fruits, along with several animals you might not see every day: an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard.

NameOfTheObject: Dutch Ships in a Calm Sea
CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.

A small boat with important people, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last supplies and crew are still being taken on board. The huge exactness in the showing of details—ropes, sails, and cannons—is striking.

NameOfTheObject: Giuliano & Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo Piero di Cosimo (1462–1522)
CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
lexical_adapted: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the base at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a two-panel painting.
persona1_adapted:
**Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516)**  
This is a portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo, an architect who lived in Italy about 500 years ago. He was the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo.  

In the painting, Giuliano is shown from the chest up, facing to the right. Behind him, you can see a landscape. At the bottom, on a small shelf, there are two tools: a compass and a pen. These tools tell us that he was a designer and builder.  

This painting is one half of a two-part artwork called a diptych. The other half is labeled SK-C-1367.
persona2_adapted:
**Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516)**  
This work depicts the architect Giuliano da Sangallo, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. The composition shows a bust portrait facing right, with a landscape visible in the background. On the plinth at the bottom of the image, a compass and a pen are placed. This panel forms part of a diptych together with SK-C-1367.
persona3_adapted:
**Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516)**  
Giuliano da Sangallo was an architect, and the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. In this portrait, we see him from the chest up, facing to the right. Behind him is a landscape scene. At the bottom of the painting, on a small ledge, there is a compass and a pen—tools that hint at his work as a designer and builder. This piece is part of a two-panel artwork (called a diptych), along with another painting labeled SK-C-1367.

**Venus and Adonis**

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
lexical_adapted: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with loose sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on each side, in the distance another warship with folded sails. Matching piece to SK-A-1848.
persona1_adapted:
**The Cannon Shot**

A warship sits quietly on calm water, its sails hanging loose. Suddenly, it fires a cannon! Two small boats float nearby, one on each side. In the distance, you can see another warship with its sails rolled up. This painting is a companion piece to another one called SK-A-1848.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

**Notes:**
- "Slack sails" = sails that are not tightened, indicating calm wind.
- "Furled sails" = sails rolled up, not in use.
- "Pendant to" = a matching or paired work (often displayed together).

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.

Wille made a name for himself by copying the kinds of everyday-life scenes that had made Greuze popular at art exhibitions in the 1760s. His work showed a preference for gentle, refined details that appealed to the tastes of the French royal court and aristocracy before the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.

persona1_adapted:
**The Asante Ewer: A Jug with Many Lives**

This fancy jug, called the Asante Ewer, was made in England a long time ago, between 1340 and 1405. But it didn't stay in England. It traveled all the way to West Africa.

In the 1880s, someone took a photo of it sitting in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asante king (called the Asantehene) in Kumasi, which is in present-day Ghana. The jug was placed in the middle of the courtyard, near what looks like a shrine, with its base tangled in the roots of sacred trees. This suggests it was a very important object for ceremonies or rituals.

Then, in 1896, during a war between the British and the Asante people (the Anglo-Asante War), British soldiers stole the jug. It was later bought by the British Museum. Another matching jug, also stolen in that same war and shown in the same photo, was given to a British army regiment.

Now, for the first time in over a hundred years, these two jugs are back together in this display.

A big new research project is studying the Asante Ewer, which is one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze work. Researchers want to learn more about how it got to Ghana, what it meant to the people there, and why it was so special.

The Asante Ewer has had a wild journey. It started as a fancy English jug, then became a sacred West African vessel, and finally ended up as war loot taken by British troops. It's amazing that it survived the long trip from Europe to Africa and back again.

During this time, drawings in this area changed in an important way. At first, artists mainly used drawings to plan their paintings or other works. But over time, people began to see drawings as finished pieces of art that were valuable on their own.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.

The very wide range and high quality of the British Museum's collection gives a special chance to present a full story of drawing from this area. Early Netherlandish drawings are very rare, as they were made as useful objects in the workshop and were not usually kept. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the love for collecting drawings in the 1500s helped more works from this time survive. Drawings were key to the design and making of art in different forms, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples show the creativity and teamwork of Netherlandish artists.

The British Museum has a huge and amazing collection of these drawings. That’s why we can show you such a complete picture of art from this region.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.

The tour will explore the preparation of the dead for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an endless restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the life after death. It will also show how burial remains can tell us about the lives of the old Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to caring for human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our rules for holding, showing and studying human remains.
persona1_adapted:
**What Was Life and Death Like in Ancient Egypt?**

Step into the world of ancient Egypt and discover how people lived, died, and what they hoped for after death. This tour will show you amazing objects like painted coffins, jewelry called amulets, small statues called shabtis, and models of daily life. You’ll also see giant wall paintings and ancient scrolls called papyri.

We’ll explore what tombs, bodies, and burial items can teach us about the lives of ancient Egyptians—and what they believed happened after they died. You’ll learn how they prepared a body for burial, including the process of mummification. You’ll also see how they filled tombs with things people might need for a peaceful, eternal rest, and how they used magic to help guide and protect the dead on their dangerous journey to the afterlife.

This tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum treats these remains with care, respect, and dignity. If you want to learn more about how we handle and study human remains, you can ask for more information.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text**

This tour examines how people in ancient Egypt lived, died, and envisioned the afterlife. It draws on a wide range of funerary objects—including human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis, tomb models, wall paintings, and papyri—to explore what tombs, burial goods, and preserved bodies reveal about both daily life and beliefs about the world beyond death.

The tour covers three main themes: the preparation of the body for burial (including mummification), the arrangement of the tomb to ensure an eternal and peaceful existence, and the use of magical practices to guide and protect the deceased on their dangerous journey to the afterlife. It also demonstrates how funerary remains can provide insights into the lives of the living.

Please note that this tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is committed to handling these materials with care, respect, and dignity. For more information, please consult our principles regarding the holding, display, and study of human remains.

**Discover Life and Death in Ancient Egypt**

You’ll see what tombs, bodies, and burial objects can teach us about the lives and beliefs of ancient Egyptians. We’ll look at how they prepared the dead for burial, including the process of mummification, how they filled tombs with items for a peaceful eternal rest, and how they used magic to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife.

The tour also shows how these burial remains reveal details about everyday life in ancient Egypt.

**Please note:** This tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is dedicated to treating these remains with care, respect, and dignity. To learn more about our principles for handling, displaying, and studying human remains, please ask a staff member or visit our website.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.

persona1_adapted:
The British Museum raised £3.5 million to save a special piece of jewelry called the Tudor Heart Pendant. Now, it will stay on display for everyone to see for many years to come.

In December 2019, someone using a metal detector in Warwickshire found this amazing heart-shaped jewel buried underground. It had been resting there for hundreds of years. The pendant is linked to King Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon.

This pendant is one of a kind—there’s nothing like it in the British Museum or anywhere else in the UK. It’s a beautifully made jewel with colorful enamel designs. It hangs from a 75-link gold chain, and the clasp is shaped like a hand coming out of a cloud.

Very few objects from Henry and Katherine’s life together still exist. That’s because their marriage was ended (annulled) in 1533. Katherine was a devoted Catholic, and Henry wanted to marry someone else. Still, their marriage was Henry’s longest—it lasted 24 years and shaped the first 30 years of his time as king.

This pendant is unlike any other object in the British Museum’s collection or elsewhere in the UK. The highly skilled jewel features enamelled motifs and is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a clasp shaped like a hand emerging from a cloud. Few surviving objects tell the story of Henry’s life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a narrative largely erased after the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union, the longest of all Henry’s marriages, lasted 24 years and profoundly shaped the first three decades of his reign.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.

persona1_adapted:
**What Were Antiquaries?**  

Back in the 1600s and 1700s, people called *antiquaries* were like history detectives. They collected old books, handwritten papers, drawings, and objects from the past. They also traveled around the country to study ruins—especially from Roman times and even earlier.  

It was pretty easy to learn about the Middle Ages (medieval times) because a lot of stuff from that period had survived. Things like religious objects, armor, books, and buildings were still around.  

But antiquaries didn’t stop there. They started to study even older places, like Stonehenge, in a more scientific way. They made maps and surveys of Roman and prehistoric sites. This helped them learn more about the very first people who lived in Britain.  

At the same time, scientists were studying rock layers (called *strata*). When you put together what the antiquaries found and what the rock layers showed, some big questions came up. For example, many people at the time believed the world was created in 4004 BC—based on the Bible. But the new evidence made archaeologists wonder if that date was really correct.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.

persona1_adapted:
**How to Make Sense of a Giant Collection**

In the 1700s, people collected tons of stuff—from coins to vases to weird tools. But if you just piled it all up, it wouldn’t make much sense. So collectors had to organize everything.

**King George III’s Library**
King George III had a huge library. Later, his son, George IV, gave it to the nation. It was kept in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (the books themselves are now in the British Library). The coins and medals from the king’s collection were some of the first things to arrive at the museum. They were sorted by country and ruler, so you could see history come alive.

**George III’s Other Interests**
The king was also really into the navy, navigation, and science. He collected instruments and working models of ships and tools.

**Sir William Hamilton’s Vases**
A man named Sir William Hamilton found ancient vases near Naples, Italy. He arranged them by the style of painting on them. This helped him figure out which ones were older and which were newer. Later, he published pictures of them in fancy books.

**Sir Hans Sloane’s Cabinets**
Earlier in the 1700s, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious manmade objects from around the world. Some of these came from enslaved people in Jamaica. He cataloged and displayed them in different ways—by type, material, date, or what they were used for.

**The Big Idea**
All this organizing helped people understand the past and the world around them.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.

Their goal was to find similarities between religions. They hoped this would help explain where the world’s religions came from. But it’s important to know that much of this research happened while European countries were taking over other lands, a time known as colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.

**Alexander the Great and the Hellenistic Age**

After Alexander died in 323 BC, his empire was split into smaller kingdoms ruled by his generals. This time period, called the **Hellenistic period**, lasted until 31 BC. That’s when Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony were defeated at a battle called Actium.

The new kingdoms were led by powerful families called dynasties. They built big buildings and supported art and culture. In Room 22 of the museum, you can see some amazing objects from this time. For example, there’s a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos. That temple was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World! You can also see a statue called the Demeter from Knidos in Turkey, and Greek bronzes.

Smaller objects on display show how skilled ancient artists were. You’ll find gold jewelry, metalwork, pottery, clay figurines, and carved gems. These items were made by goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, and gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms

**Africa at the Museum**

The British Museum has lots of amazing objects from Africa. You can find them in Room 25 (the Africa gallery), the Sainsbury Galleries, and in other parts of the museum too. These objects show Africa’s rich history and cultures.

Want to learn more? Join one of our free tours! These tours were created with help from people in the community. Each tour guide picks their own favorite objects to show you. The objects come from ancient times all the way to today.

You can choose from these tour themes:

- **The Igbo World** – Artifacts from the Igbo people of Nigeria, now at the British Museum
- **African Metal Art** – How metal objects were made, traded, and used in tradition
- **Ancient African Kingdoms** – Objects from powerful kingdoms long ago

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
