The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on show in Room 9. The panels show a variety of scenes, including the moving of huge statues of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were meant for the main entrances to the palace. These pictures give a look into old stone-cutting and moving methods, as well as Sennacherib's strong interest in his building projects. Other panels on show show the king's war campaigns.

Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704 to 681 BC. He was famous for his huge building projects. His palace in Nineveh, called the Southwest Palace, was decorated with detailed stone carvings. Many of these carvings are on display in Room 9.

The carvings show different scenes. One scene shows workers moving giant stone statues of human-headed winged bulls, called *lamassu*. These statues weighed up to 30 tons and were placed at the main entrances to the palace. The carvings give us a look at how ancient people cut and moved heavy stone. They also show how much Sennacherib cared about his building projects.

Other carvings in the room show the king's military campaigns.

They depict various subjects, including the transport of enormous sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu), each weighing up to 30 tons. These figures were originally placed at the main entrances of the palace. The reliefs offer valuable insight into ancient quarrying and transportation methods, as well as Sennacherib’s personal interest in his construction projects. Other panels in the room illustrate the king’s military campaigns.

The rooms and courtyards of his Southwest Palace in Nineveh were decorated with detailed stone carvings. Many of these carvings are now on display in Room 9. They show a variety of scenes, including the movement of enormous stone statues of human-headed winged bulls, called *lamassu*. These statues weighed up to 30 tons and were made for the main entrances of the palace. The carvings give us a look into how the ancient Assyrians quarried and moved heavy stone, and they also show how interested Sennacherib was in his building work. Other carvings in the room show the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad. CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, together with carvings showing the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. persona1_adapted:
**The Palace of King Sargon II**

In ancient times, a powerful king named Sargon II ruled the Assyrian Empire. He built a huge city and palace at a place called Khorsabad, which is in modern-day northern Iraq. This was around 2,700 years ago.

To protect his palace, Sargon placed giant statues at the entrances. These were massive stone creatures that looked like bulls but had the heads of humans and wings on their backs. They were meant to be magical guardians, watching over the palace and keeping evil away.

Today, you can see two of these amazing statues in Room 10c. They are very tall and impressive! Around them are stone carvings that show the king, his son (the crown prince), royal servants, and scenes of hunting.

Nearby, there are also some old stone writings (called inscriptions) that came from a similar winged bull statue. This one was from the palace of another Assyrian king, Sennacherib, who ruled after Sargon. His palace was in a city called Nineveh. These inscriptions are badly burned because the city was destroyed in a war around 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.

**Ancient Anatolia and Urartu: A Bridge Between Worlds**

Long ago, the lands of Anatolia and Urartu connected Europe and Asia. Today, these areas are part of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwest Iran.

In the Early Bronze Age (thousands of years ago), skilled artists made beautiful objects. You can see a silver bull and a silver cup from this time.

Later, during the Middle Bronze Age, merchants kept records of their business deals. These old documents show us how people traded between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq).

During the Hittite period, artists created delicate gold jewelry and small statues.

From the Iron Age, the Urartu people made amazing furniture decorations. Look for winged bulls and griffins—mythical creatures that were part animal, part bird.

Iron Age objects from Urartu include winged bulls and griffins, which were used as decorative elements on furniture.

Records from Middle Bronze Age merchants show how trade linked central Anatolia with Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). From the Hittite period, you can see delicate gold jewelry and small statues. Objects from the Iron Age Urartu culture include winged bulls and griffins that were used to decorate furniture.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
lexical_adapted: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite large statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing royal head-cloth and circle of sacred snakes (about half now lost), the sculptor has used the two-color nature of the stone to highlight the division between body and face; the back pillar is carved with vertical lines of picture-writing - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of color upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in ancient times.
persona1_adapted:
This is a giant statue of Ramses II, made from pink and gray granite. It was one of a pair that once stood in front of a temple called the Ramesseum. The statue shows the king’s head and upper body. He wears a royal headdress called a *nemes* and a crown of cobras (called *uraei*), though about half of the cobras are now missing.

The sculptor used the two colors of the stone on purpose. The gray part is the face, and the pink part is the body. This helps the face stand out. On the back pillar, there are rows of hieroglyphs carved into the stone. They tell the king’s name and titles, and part of a prayer to the god Amun-Ra.

In 1817, people noticed that there were still traces of paint on the statue. That means it was probably painted red long ago.

**Adapted Text:**

The king wears the *nemes* headdress and a circlet of *uraei* (sacred cobras), though about half of this circlet is now missing.

The sculptor made clever use of the stone’s two-tone (bichrome) quality to clearly separate the face from the rest of the body. On the back pillar, vertical rows of hieroglyphs record the king’s names and titles, along with part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra.

In 1817, traces of paint were still visible on the statue, suggesting that in ancient times it may have been painted red.

In 1817, people noted that there were still traces of color on the statue. This means it was likely painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.

**Goddess Tara**  
This is a small statue of a goddess named Tara. She was made from bronze and covered with a thin layer of gold.  

Tara has a tall, tube-shaped hairdo held in place with a fancy medallion. On either side of the medallion are *makaras*—mythical water creatures. The medallion and Tara’s eyes were probably decorated with jewels at one time.  

Her earlobes are long and stretched. She wears a tight cloth tied at her hips, but her upper body is bare.  

Her right hand is held in a special pose called *varadamudra*, which means she is offering a gift or blessing. Her left hand is in *katakahastamudra*—the tips of her fingers touch her thumb to form a ring, like she’s holding a flower.  

Some parts of the statue are missing: the two middle fingers on her right hand and some toes from both feet.
persona2_adapted:

---

**Adapted Text:**

This is a standing figure of a female deity, identified as Tārā. It is solid-cast in bronze and gilded. The figure wears a tall, tubular hairstyle (jatamukuta), which is held in place by a medallion. On either side of the medallion are makaras (mythical water creatures). The medallion was likely set with precious stones, and the eyes were also probably inlaid with stones. The earlobes are elongated. The lower body is covered by a tight-fitting cloth, knotted at the hips, while the upper body is bare. The right hand is shown in varadamudrā (the gesture of giving). The left hand is in katakahastamudrā (a hand position where the tips of the fingers touch the thumb, forming a ring, as if holding a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing, as are some toes from both feet.

---

**Notes on changes made:**

- **"High tubular coiffure"** → **"tall, tubular hairstyle"**: "Coiffure" is a less common word; "hairstyle" is clearer.
- **"Flanked by"** → **"On either side of"**: More direct and easier to visualize.
- **"Water creatures"** → **"mythical water creatures"**: Added "mythical" to clarify the cultural context.
- **"Probably set with stones"** → **"likely set with precious stones"**: "Stones" can be ambiguous; "precious stones" is more specific.
- **"Proper right hand"** → **"right hand"**: The term "proper right" (viewer's left) can confuse non-native speakers; "right hand" is sufficient here.
- **"The hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring"** → **"a hand position where the tips of the fingers touch the thumb, forming a ring"**: Simplified sentence structure and vocabulary.
- **"Missing as are toes from both feet"** → **"missing, as are some toes from both feet"**: Added "some" for clarity; original phrasing could imply all toes are missing.

The center of the band was probably once filled with precious stones, and the eyes of the statue were likely decorated with stones too.

Her earlobes are stretched long, and she wears a tight cloth wrapped around her lower body, tied at the hips. Her upper body is bare. In her right hand, she makes a gesture of giving. In her left hand, her thumb and fingertips touch to form a ring, as if she is holding a flower. Two of the fingers on her right hand are missing, and some toes are also gone from both feet.

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
lexical_adapted: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn together. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona1_adapted:
This cloth is a type of fabric called **adire eleko**, which is a special kind of indigo-dyed cloth from Nigeria. The designs are made using a "resist" method—meaning parts of the cloth are protected from the dye to create patterns.

The fabric is made from cotton that was bought from a factory, and two large pieces have been sewn together by machine. Each piece is divided into a grid of 4 rows and 7 columns, making 28 squares in total.

The patterns were painted by hand. In two of the squares, you can see the pillars of **Mapo Hall**, a famous building in the city of Ibadan, Nigeria. Between those pillars, there are four shapes that look like pestles (tools used for grinding) or spoons.
persona2_adapted: The cloth is an indigo resist-dyed textile, known as *adire eleko*. It is made from factory-produced cotton, with two panels machine-sewn together. Each panel is divided into a grid of four by seven squares. The patterns are hand-painted and include two squares featuring the pillars of Mapo Hall (located in Ibadan), with four pestles (or spoons) placed between them.
persona3_adapted: This cloth was made using a traditional Nigerian technique called *adire eleko*, where a starchy paste is painted onto the fabric to resist the dye. The cloth is made of two panels of factory-made cotton that have been sewn together by machine. Each panel is divided into a grid of four rows and seven squares. The patterns were painted by hand. In two of the squares, you can see the pillars of Mapo Hall, a famous building in the city of Ibadan. Between these pillars are four shapes that look like pestles (used for grinding) or spoons.

On the left, you see the painter Richard Wilson. He is carrying a painting to a pawn shop, where he will sell it for quick cash. On the right is the writer Daniel Defoe, author of *Robinson Crusoe*. He has a copy of his famous manuscript in his pocket, but he looks sad as he leaves the office of his publisher, Edmund Curll. Defoe’s worried wife and daughter are waiting for him. In the foreground (the front of the scene), you can see books and a painter’s palette with brushes lying on the ground.

This print was made in 1851 using a mix of printing techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It was printed on a special thin paper called *chine collé*, which is glued onto a stronger backing sheet.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
lexical_adapted: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are cut in. The main band shows two griffins separated by a tree-like pattern; a lion fills the space between the two mythical beasts; filling patterns in the background. Above the main scene is a band of flower-like designs; below simpler patterns made of dots separated by S-shaped lines. Simple lines on the vase, and tongue shapes on the lip.
persona1_adapted:
This small, round pot is called an **alabastron** (say: al-uh-BASS-tron). It was made in ancient Greece, in a region called Protocorinthia. People used it to hold perfumed oil.

The pot was shaped on a potter’s wheel. It has a tiny mouth with a slanted rim, and a little handle shaped like a button on top. The clay is very fine and smooth, and the artist painted black decorations on it. They also added purple paint on the animals’ bodies and carved fine lines to show details like fur and feathers.

The main picture around the pot shows two **griffins** (mythical creatures with the head and wings of an eagle and the body of a lion). Between them is a tree-like design. A **lion** stands in the space between the two griffins. Small extra shapes fill the empty areas.

Above the main scene is a band of flower-like patterns. Below that are simple dots separated by S-shaped lines. The bottom of the pot has simple rays (like sunbeams), and the rim has tongue-shaped decorations.
persona2_adapted:
Here is the adapted version of the text, tailored for a non-native English speaker with deep art knowledge.

---

**Adapted Text:**

This Protocorinthian alabastron is a small, wheel-made vessel used for holding perfumed oil. Its body is ovoid (egg-shaped) and slightly flattened, resting on a rounded base. The mouth is small with a sloping rim, and there is a pierced lug handle at the top.

The vessel is made of fine buff clay with a slip (a thin layer of liquid clay). The painted decoration has fired black, with additional purple paint applied to the bodies of the animals. The details of the figures are incised (cut into the surface).

The main decorative band (frieze) shows two griffins (mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head of an eagle). Between them is a stylized tree-like motif. A lion occupies the space between the two griffins. The field around the figures is filled with smaller decorative motifs (filling ornaments).

Above the main scene, there is a frieze of stylized floral designs. Below this, there are simpler motifs made of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. The vase also has simple rays (radiating lines) on its body, and tongues (tongue-shaped patterns) on the lip.
persona3_adapted:
This small, rounded pottery bottle, called an alabastron, was made in ancient Greece around the 7th century BCE. It was used to hold perfumed oil. The vase was shaped on a potter's wheel and has a small mouth with a sloping rim, a rounded base, and a small handle on the side.

The decoration is painted in black and purple on a pale clay background. Some details of the animals are scratched into the surface. The main scene shows a lion and two griffins—mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head and wings of an eagle. They are separated by a simple, tree-like design. Small decorative shapes fill the empty spaces around them.

Above this scene is a band of flower-like patterns. Below are simpler designs made of dots and S-shaped lines. The bottom of the vase has simple lines, and the rim is decorated with small tongue-like shapes.

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
lexical_adapted:
IWM has kept a Film Archive for over a hundred years. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as first-hand source material and its large collection shows the causes, course and results of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A great resource for researchers and film-makers.
Our always growing collection now reaches over 25,000 hours of moving images, showing a wide and varied range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two large collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also kept. An ongoing film digitisation programme gives free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.

Our collection keeps growing and now has over 25,000 hours of moving images. It includes a wide range of materials, such as public information films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and home movies. We also have two large collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. We are working to digitize these films so people can watch them online for free, and we add new digital copies as soon as they are ready.

IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.

These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen. IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which shows the day-to-day running of the war art programs and gives interesting looks at the artists' experiences.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)

In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors lit against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background show further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was fitted with 6" guns in 1939 and turned into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he stayed with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original right afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a traveling show of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona1_adapted:
**The Sinking of HMS Laurentic**

This painting shows the moment a ship called HMS Laurentic was sinking. You can see half of the ship underwater, with its front end sticking up into the air. In the front of the painting, a lifeboat full of sailors is lit up by a small light in the dark. Another lifeboat is on the right, and tiny lights in the background show even more lifeboats. On the left, you can just see the top of a German submarine (called a U-boat).

HMS Laurentic started out as a passenger ship for a company called P&O. In 1939, it was turned into a warship by adding big guns. This was the first ship that the artist, Frank Worsley, served on. He stayed with it until it was sunk off the coast of Ulster (in Ireland) by a German U-boat captain named Otto Kretschmer on the night of November 3, 1940.

Worsley painted the first version of this picture right after the sinking, while he was on leave. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought the painting. In August 1942, they sent it on a tour to South America. But the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed and sank! So Worsley painted this replacement version at the WAAC’s request.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This image shows HMS Laurentic sinking in the background. Half of the ship is underwater, and its bow rises above the surface. In the foreground, a lifeboat filled with sailors is lit by a small light against the dark water. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and small points of light in the background indicate more lifeboats. On the left side of the background, the top of a German U-boat is visible.

HMS Laurentic was originally a passenger liner for the P&O company. In 1939, it was equipped with 6-inch guns and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. This ship was Worsley’s first command, and he stayed with it until it was sunk off Foreland, Ulster, by German U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940.

Worsley painted the original version immediately after the event, while on survivor’s leave. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) purchased the painting and sent it to South America in August 1942 as part of a traveling exhibition of war art. However, during transport, the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed. The present work was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC.

*(Source: Exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)*
persona3_adapted:
**The Sinking of HMS *Laurentic***  
*Painted by Worsley*  

In this painting, you can see the British ship *HMS Laurentic* sinking in the background. Half of the ship is underwater, and its front end (the bow) is sticking up into the air. In the front of the picture, a lifeboat is filled with sailors. They are lit up against the dark night by a small light. Another lifeboat appears on the right side, and tiny dots of light in the distance show there are more lifeboats. On the left side, you can just make out the top of a German submarine (a U-boat).  

*HMS Laurentic* was originally a passenger ship for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, it was fitted with large guns and turned into an armed merchant cruiser—a warship that could also carry cargo. This was the first ship that the artist, Worsley, served on. He stayed with it until it was sunk off the coast of Foreland, in Ulster, by a German U-boat captain named Otto Kretschmer (who commanded U-99) on the night of November 3, 1940.  

Worsley painted the original version of this scene right after the attack, while he was on leave as a survivor. The War Artists' Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought that painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a traveling exhibition of war art. But the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed on the way, and the original was lost. The WAAC then asked Worsley to paint this replacement, which is the picture you see here.  

*(Based on information from the 1995 exhibition catalog, Burstow Gallery, Brighton)*

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. persona1_adapted:
**A Drawing That Changed Everything**

In the summer of 1983, artist Eric Rimmington was drawing an old train station in London. He planned to make ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. But while working on the second drawing, he made a shocking discovery.

The train tracks he was drawing were used several times a week to carry nuclear waste from a power plant called Sizewell to another facility called Sellafield. This fact changed everything for Rimmington. For the next six weeks, he couldn't stop thinking about it. Every mark he made in his drawings was connected to that scary truth.

**A Time of Fear and Protest**

Rimmington created these drawings during a period when many people were protesting against nuclear weapons and nuclear power. In 1979, the British government agreed to let American Cruise Missiles be placed in the UK. This decision sparked huge marches and protests. One famous protest was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which started in 1981 and was at its peak in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived.

That same year, 1979, a serious accident happened at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA. This accident made people even more afraid of nuclear power.

**Still a Concern Today**

Nuclear waste—and how it is transported and stored—remains a big worry for many people. In fact, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly asked for a special report on the safety of nuclear waste trains. So the issues Rimmington drew about are still with us today.

(Rimmington, from the 1987 exhibition catalogue *'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing'*)

NameOfTheObject: Evacuees in a Cottage at Cookham Children in Wartime - Five lithographs by Ethel Gabain
CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).

**Label**  
This is Mrs. Norris from Cookham, with five of the seven kids from London who were staying with her during World War II. By January 1941, they had been living with her for sixteen months. On the table, there is a large "Spotted Dick" pudding (a traditional British steamed suet pudding with dried fruit), along with several bowls and utensils. The woman is serving a boy on the far right, who holds out a bowl to receive his portion.

**Label**  
"Mrs. Norris, from Cookham, is pictured with five of the seven children from London who were part of her 'war family.' By January 1941, these children had been living with her for sixteen months. Mrs. Norris represents the ideal foster parent, and many other householders like her existed at the time. Over a million children had been moved from evacuation areas in organized school groups. Given such large numbers, placing children in private homes (billets) was the main way to provide them with accommodation. Thanks to Mrs. Norris’s warmth and care, city children quickly adapted to their new surroundings." (Title from folder cover: *Children in Wartime*)

**Notes for the reader:**  
- *Spotted Dick*: a traditional English pudding; the name may sound unusual but refers to a dessert with dried fruit ("spots").  
- *Billets*: private homes where evacuees were temporarily housed.  
- *Evacuation areas*: cities considered at risk of bombing during World War II, from which children were sent to safer rural locations.  
- The cultural context here is the British wartime evacuation program (1939–1945), which moved children from urban areas to the countryside for safety.

This picture shows Mrs. Norris of Cookham, along with five of the seven children from London who were staying with her during World War II. The original caption describes Mrs. Norris as an ideal foster parent, and notes that many other families like hers were doing the same thing.

Because there were so many children, placing them in private homes was the main way to find them shelter. Thanks to the kindness and care of people like Mrs. Norris, city children were able to adjust well to their new surroundings.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. persona1_adapted:
**Portrait of a Factory Worker**

This painting shows a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a large machine called a lathe. She was only 21 years old and had never worked in a factory before. But she was so good at her job that the War Artist's Advisory Committee called her "an outstanding factory worker."

The artist, Laura Knight, first planned to paint Loftus in a studio. But the Ministry of Supply asked her to paint Loftus at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport, Wales. There, Loftus was making a part for a Bofors gun called a breech ring. This was the most difficult job in the factory—it usually took eight or nine years of training to learn. Loftus mastered it in a very short time.

Some people think Loftus was put in front of the machine just for the painting, to make a good impression. But either way, her skill was real. At the time, it was rare for a woman to do such complex factory work, and her success was a big deal for the war effort.

Laura Knight had never painted industrial machinery before. But she got the details right, and critics praised her accuracy. Like Loftus, Knight was proving that women could succeed in jobs that were usually done by men.

Knight was also known for painting circus performers and dancers. In this portrait, you can see that same focus on balance and posture—how Loftus stands and moves as she works the machine.

CuratorialText:
image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.

image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the back and front parts above water. The front part is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke rising up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast heading for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was held by a British force. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was traveling to check the defenses of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the force. At 11.45pm the Eclipse hit a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's crew of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue boat of the RAF sent from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was among the survivors. He went on to become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, leaving the Army in 1949. He wrote down this terrible event 24 years after it had happened.
persona1_adapted:
**A Ship Sinks in the Night**

This painting shows the British destroyer HMS *Eclipse* sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship has broken in two, with only the front and back ends sticking out of the water. The front part is on fire, sending a bright cloud of flames and smoke into the night sky. Another ship is in the background, and two Greek islands can be seen on the left and right.

**What Happened**

On October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—were sailing along the coast of Turkey. Their destination was the Greek island of Leros, which was held by British soldiers. On board the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking officer on his way to inspect the island's defenses. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., disaster struck. The *Eclipse* hit a mine (an underwater bomb) between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The ship exploded and sank in just two minutes. In addition to its crew of about 200 men, the ship was carrying another 200 soldiers. Only about 100 people survived. They were rescued by a small RAF boat and by boats from the *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the cold water.

Brigadier-General Davy was one of the survivors. He later became the commander of land forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He recorded this terrifying event 24 years after it happened.

**Adapted Text:**

He was traveling to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships also carried troops, ammunition, and supplies for the garrison.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* struck a naval mine between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The explosion caused the ship to break apart and sink in only two minutes. In addition to its regular crew of about 200 men, the ship was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company from the Buffs (a British infantry regiment). Only about 100 men survived. They were rescued by an air-sea rescue launch sent from Leros and by boats from HMS *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

Brigadier-General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.

**Notes for the reader:**
- *The Buffs*: A historical nickname for the Royal East Kent Regiment, a British infantry unit.
- *Air-sea rescue launch*: A small, fast boat used to rescue people from the water.
- *Garrison*: A group of soldiers stationed in a specific location for defense.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.

persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This drawing shows a jumble of twisted metal beams of all different sizes. It’s not a picture of a building—it’s what’s left after bombs hit. The artist, Paule Vézelay, wanted to show how explosions twist and break structures. You can almost feel the force of the blast in the way the beams bend and curve.

Vézelay was born in Bristol, England. She spent more than 10 years living in Paris as an artist, but when World War II started, she moved back home. Because of her political views (she was left-wing) and because her art was abstract (not realistic), she never got an official job as a war artist. But with help from the Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she got permission to draw the bombed-out ruins in her hometown.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.

The Labyrinth was a clever maze ordered by King Minos to trap the fierce Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous half-god hero. After Theseus's win, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths came from stories which the Greek and Roman people of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Around 4,000 years ago, the island of Crete was home to a group of people called the Minoans. They lived long before the Greeks took over.

In 1900, an archaeologist named Sir Arthur Evans started digging at a place called Knossos on Crete. As he uncovered old buildings and objects, he noticed they reminded him of ancient stories from Crete. He named the main building the “Palace of Minos,” after a mythical king.

According to the legend, King Minos ordered a giant maze called the Labyrinth to be built. Inside, he trapped a terrifying monster called the Minotaur. This creature had the head of a bull and the body of a man. The hero Theseus, who was part god, went into the maze, killed the Minotaur, and escaped. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and founded the city of Athens.

Even after the palace was destroyed, the people who lived in Knossos—first Greeks and later Romans—kept telling these stories for many generations.

The Labyrinth was an intricate maze that King Minos ordered to be built in order to imprison the fierce Minotaur—a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. According to the myth, the Minotaur was killed by Theseus, a famous semi-divine hero. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and was regarded as the founder of Athens. He named the building he found the "Palace of Minos," after the legendary king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. 

According to the myth, King Minos ordered the construction of a clever maze called the Labyrinth. Its purpose was to trap the Minotaur—a terrifying creature with a bull's head and a human body. CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the rise of the landscape print. Especially important was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this bright color gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the showing of sky and water.

Here we show a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

In this gallery, you'll see some of the best landscape prints by the master Utagawa Hiroshige, along with selected works by two other famous artists, Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. NameOfTheObject: View of the High Street, Oxford
CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.

persona1_adapted:
This painting started as a special job. A publisher wanted two watercolors to use in a yearly calendar called the *Oxford Almanack*. The artist, J.M.W. Turner, did such a good job that he ended up making ten watercolors for them between 1798 and 1804.

Later, a local art dealer named James Wyatt asked Turner to turn this watercolor and another one into oil paintings. Turner even asked Wyatt for advice on the details of the buildings in the scene. Turner also added people to the painting—university members, church leaders, and some women—just to make the colors look better.

We know a lot about this painting because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him. Those letters were sold after Wyatt died. The original plan was to make an engraving (a printed copy) of the design. But instead, Wyatt decided to buy an oil painting. It was half the size of Turner’s usual paintings, and it cost 100 guineas (a lot of money back then).

The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. Years later, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing scenes of Oxford for his most famous series of watercolors. He didn’t pick the High Street again. He felt that in this painting, he had already created a perfect view—one so technically amazing that he couldn’t do it again.

He also added figures—members of the University and clergy—as well as some women, “for the sake of colour.”

This painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works. The reason is that Wyatt kept the correspondence he had with the artist, and these letters were included in Wyatt’s posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. However, Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolour. The canvas was half the size of Turner’s usual works, and the cost was 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812.

Later, he turned this work and one other into oil paintings for a local print seller and frame maker named James Wyatt.

Turner asked Wyatt for advice on the details of the buildings shown in the scene. He felt that in this painting, he had already achieved a level of technical skill that he could not match a second time.

Medium: tempera and oil, with traces of gold, on panel
CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.

persona1_adapted:
**The Hunt in the Forest: A Nighttime Adventure**

Imagine a dark forest at night, filled with hunters, dogs, and deer. That’s the scene in *The Hunt in the Forest*, a painting by Paolo di Dono—better known as Uccello (which means “Bird” in Italian). This artwork is special because it shows a hunt happening at night, which was unusual for its time. Hunting was a fancy hobby for rich people, but a nighttime hunt? That’s playful and might be more about imagination than real life.

Uccello was a master of making things look three-dimensional on a flat surface. To do this, he first drew a grid on the wooden panel and placed a single point in the center to pull your eyes deep into the scene. He added clever details—like the hunters’ spears, cut branches, logs, and water in the front—to guide your gaze toward the people and animals fading into the distance.

The painting was meant to be seen by candlelight. Tiny gold flakes in the leaves would have sparkled and seemed to move as the candles flickered, making the dark forest come alive. Uccello was famous for his skill with perspective, animals, and landscapes. He also worked on mosaics and stained glass, and you can see all those talents combined in this masterpiece.

*The Hunt in the Forest* is a type of painting called a *spalliera* (say: spah-lee-AIR-ah). It was hung at shoulder height, either on the back of a fancy chest or set into wooden wall panels. We don’t know who paid for it, but it was definitely made for a grand Renaissance palace—maybe in Urbino, where Uccello worked in 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.

NameOfTheObject: Still Life with a Faience Figure
CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

The earthenware double salt holder, a type made in large numbers in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. NameOfTheObject: St Nicholas of Bari banishing the Storm
CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
lexical_adapted: This panel comes from the base of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now spread in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the center was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The base showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas bringing back to life three youths; the saint providing marriage money for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional symbol); and calming the storm. The panels probably first had four-lobed frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona1_adapted:
This panel was once part of a larger artwork called an altarpiece, made between 1433 and 1435 for a church in Florence, Italy. The altarpiece was broken up over time, and its pieces are now in different museums around the world.

The main part of the altarpiece showed Mary holding baby Jesus, surrounded by four angels. On either side were pairs of saints, including Saint Benedict, Saint Nicholas, John the Baptist, and another saint (probably Saint Matthew). 

The bottom section, called the predella, told stories from the life of Saint Nicholas. Five of these story panels still exist today. They show:
- The birth of Saint Nicholas
- Pilgrims visiting his shrine
- Saint Nicholas bringing three young people back to life
- Saint Nicholas giving money to three poor sisters (this is why he is often shown with three gold balls)
- Saint Nicholas calming a storm

The panels probably had a four-leaf clover shape around them. The artist, Bicci, based his design on an earlier altarpiece by another famous artist, Gentile da Fabriano.

The predella illustrated scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas, of which five are known today: the saint's birth; pilgrims at his shrine; his revival of three youths; his provision of dowries for three sisters (symbolized by three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and his calming of a storm. The panels likely originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the composition of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altarpiece of 1425, created for the church of San Niccolò Oltrarno.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
lexical_adapted: The beginning of this painting started in 1857, when it was shown as The Mother's Grave. The first version, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy sad over the grave of his mother. The landscape was started in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes changed the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to strengthen the sadness of the subject, so that the short-lived nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all highlight the theme of passing time. The boy's loss is told again in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.

This painting started out in 1857 with the title *The Mother's Grave*. At first, it only showed a sad boy standing by his mother's grave. We know this from an early sketch that's now in the Ashmolean Museum.

The artist, Arthur Hughes, began painting the outdoor scene in the summer of 1856 in an old churchyard in Chingford, Essex. About five years later, around 1862, he changed the background and added the boy's sister. His wife, Tryphena, posed as the model for the sister.

Hughes filled the painting with small details that make you feel the sadness of loss. Look closely at the spiderwebs, dewdrops, wild roses, and dandelion seeds. All these things are delicate and don't last long—just like life itself. Even the lamb on the other side of the tomb is separated from its mother, reminding us of the boy's own loss.

NameOfTheObject: A Woman Seated at the Piano
CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

But there’s no other reason to think she’s a nun. In fact, it’s almost certainly a portrait of the artist’s daughter, Desirée-Marie Ribot (known as Louise). The artist often used her as a model. She was born in 1857, and her young face helps us guess when the painting was made—probably in the late 1860s or early 1870s. That’s before the artist’s painting style became thicker and more textured.

The dark background and the simple, homey scene remind us of the work of two other artists: François Bonvin and Alphonse Legros. Bonvin was an early supporter of Ribot, and both Bonvin and Legros were interested in Spanish art.

NameOfTheObject: The Feast in the House of Levi
CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Many copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a smaller copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.

To avoid trouble, Veronese simply changed the title of the painting to *The Feast in the House of Levi*—a story from the Bible that allowed for more colorful and lively scenes.

The original painting is very large and now hangs in a museum in Venice. Many artists made copies of it. The painting in this museum (WA1855.159) is a smaller, high-quality copy made by a Venetian artist around the mid-1700s, most likely Francesco Fontebasso.

This new subject allowed him to keep the many colourful, non-religious details in the scene (based on Luke 6:27-30).

The original large painting is now in the Accademia Gallery in Venice, and many copies of it exist. Our painting (WA1855.159) is a smaller, high-quality copy made by a Venetian artist from the mid-18th century, most likely Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.

persona1_adapted:
**A Father and Son Paint the Same View**

This painting was made in 1886, when Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien were both using a technique called pointillism. That’s where you paint with tiny dots of color instead of brushstrokes.

The scene shows a bend in a path near Éragny, a village in France. In the middle of the painting, you can see the spire (the tall, pointy top) of the Saint-Martin church. On the right is a brick manor house from the 1500s. A river called the Epte runs behind the line of trees that cuts across the picture.

In 1887, Lucien painted almost the same view, but in watercolor. If you compare the two paintings, you’ll notice something interesting: Lucien made the church and manor look much bigger than they really were. He also left out some farm buildings that stood in front of the manor. Today, trees hide most of these buildings from that spot on the path.

Lucien probably knew that his father had painted the exact same view from the same place in 1884. But Camille’s version used a looser, more spontaneous style—the way he painted in the early 1880s. So even though father and son stood in the same spot, they saw the scene in very different ways.

**Adapted Text:**

Painted in 1886, this work was created when both Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien were using the pointillist technique. The scene is taken from a bend in the path that leads from the Pré du Chêne, across the river Epte, from Éragny to Bazincourt. In the center of the composition, we see the spire of the church of Saint-Martin in Éragny, and on the right, a sixteenth-century brick manor house. The river Epte runs along the line of trees that cuts across the image.

In 1887, Lucien painted a nearly identical view in watercolor and bodycolor. Today, the buildings visible from this part of the path are mostly hidden by trees. However, it is clear that in both works, Lucien enlarged the church and the manor significantly, while suppressing the farm buildings that stand in front of the manor. As Lucien must have known, his father had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more spontaneous style of the early 1880s.

The scene shows a view from a bend in a path that leads from a place called Pré du Chêne, across the Epte River, from the village of Éragny to Bazincourt. In the center of the painting, you can see the spire of the Saint-Martin church in Éragny, and on the right, a brick manor house built in the 1500s. NameOfTheObject:
Village of Lauris in the Vaucluse
Village of Lauris in Vaucluse, on the Banks of the River Durance (popular)
CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
